For each element, binary search tails for the first value ≥ current and replace it.
Length of tails array at the end is the LIS length.
title: Longest Increasing Sequence in Design Versions

description: A 3D mesh is modeled as a flow network. Each edge has a capacity. Find the maximum flow from source s to sink t (which equals the minimum cut by Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem). Implement Ford-Fulkerson or Edmonds-Karp.
explanation: Max flow = 4 (paths: 0→1→3=2, 0→2→3=2, 0→1→2→3 gets blocked). Min cut = 4.
hints: Use BFS (Edmonds-Karp) to find augmenting paths in the residual graph.
Build residual graph with forward and backward edges.
Each BFS finds a shortest augmenting path from source to sink.
Augment along the path by the minimum capacity edge.
Repeat until no augmenting path exists; total flow augmented is the max flow.
title: Minimum Cut for Mesh Segmentation

description: In Autodesk's grid-based floor planning, place n components on an n×n grid such that no two components share the same row, column, or diagonal (analogous to N-Queens). Return all distinct valid placements.
explanation: There are 2 distinct solutions for 4-Queens: [[.Q..,...],[...] etc.
hints: Place one component per row using backtracking.
Track which columns and diagonals are occupied using boolean arrays.
Two diagonals: left-diagonal (row-col) and right-diagonal (row+col).
When column c is safe for current row r, place and recurse to row r+1.
Backtrack by unplacing and trying next column.
title: N-Queens for Grid Layout Planning

description: Implement a 2D range sum query system. Given a 2D matrix of terrain elevation values, answer multiple queries of the form: sum of all values in the rectangle from (r1,c1) to (r2,c2). Precompute to answer each query in O(1).
explanation: Sum of submatrix rows 0-1, cols 0-1: 3+0+5+6=14.
hints: Build 2D prefix sum: prefix[i][j] = sum of all elements in rectangle (0,0) to (i-1,j-1).
prefix[i][j] = matrix[i-1][j-1] + prefix[i-1][j] + prefix[i][j-1] - prefix[i-1][j-1].
Query (r1,c1,r2,c2) = prefix[r2+1][c2+1] - prefix[r1][c2+1] - prefix[r2+1][c1] + prefix[r1][c1].
This is the 2D inclusion-exclusion principle.
Precompute prefix once, answer each query in O(1).
title: 2D Region Sum for Terrain Analysis

constraints:
1 ≤ nums.length ≤ 5000
-5000 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 5000
All values are unique.
description: An array of version IDs was originally sorted but has been rotated at an unknown pivot. Find the minimum element (rotation point index) in O(log n). All elements are distinct.
explanation: Minimum is 0 at index 4. Binary search: if nums[mid] > nums[right], minimum is in right half; else in left half.
hints: Use binary search with lo=0 and hi=n-1.
Compare nums[mid] with nums[hi].
If nums[mid] > nums[hi], the minimum is in the right half: lo = mid+1.
Else the minimum is in the left half (including mid): hi = mid.
When lo == hi, that index is the minimum.
title: Find Rotation Point in Sorted Drawing History

description: Implement a circular queue for incident ticket processing. Support: enqueue(ticket), dequeue() → returns and removes front ticket, peek() → returns front without removing, isEmpty(), and isFull().
explanation: Dequeue returns front in FIFO order: 1 then 2.
sampleInput: capacity=3, enqueue(1), enqueue(2), dequeue(), enqueue(3), enqueue(4), dequeue()

testCases.input: capacity=3, enqueue(1), enqueue(2), dequeue(), enqueue(3), enqueue(4), dequeue()
capacity=3, enqueue(1), enqueue(2), dequeue(), enqueue(3), enqueue(4), dequeue()
capacity=5, enqueue(1), enqueue(2), enqueue(3), dequeue(), dequeue()

title: Incident Ticket Processing Order

description: Given an array of incident category strings, return the category that appears most frequently. If there are ties, return the lexicographically smallest one.
explanation: network:3, hardware:2, software:1. Most common is 'network'.
title: Most Common Incident Category

description: You have n meetings each with a start time and end time. Find the maximum number of non-overlapping meetings you can attend (each meeting attended in full). Return the maximum count.
explanation: Choose [1,3],[3,5],[... wait [3,5] and [4,6] overlap. Choose [1,3],[3,5] — do they overlap? end of first = start of second = 3, non-overlapping if strict. 3 meetings: [1,3],[3,5] wait still only 2. [1,3],[4,6] = 2. [2,4],[4,6]=2. Actually: [1,3],[3,5] if [start,end) then yes 3 meetings max... With strict intervals [1,3],[3,5] don't overlap → can attend both. Then [5,?] doesn't exist. Max=2. Hmm, re-order: [1,3],[3,5] = 2; adding [4,6] overlaps with [3,5]. Answer = 2.
hints: Sort meetings by end time.
Greedily select the meeting that ends earliest.
Skip any meeting that starts before the last selected meeting ends.
This greedy choice leaves maximum room for future meetings.
title: Optimal SLA Meeting Schedule

description: Implement serialization and deserialization of a binary tree representing a hierarchical incident structure. Serialize the tree to a string and deserialize it back to the original tree. Use any format you choose.
explanation: BFS-based serialization stores nodes level by level with null markers for missing children.
hints: Use BFS for serialization: process level by level, mark null children.
For deserialization, rebuild using a queue: pop parent, assign next two values as children.
Skip 'null' markers (they don't get children).
Alternatively use preorder DFS serialization.
title: Serialize and Deserialize Incident Tree

constraints:
0 ≤ s.length ≤ 5×10^4
s consists of English letters, digits, symbols, and spaces
description: ServiceNow error codes are single characters. Given a string of error codes, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
explanation: Longest substring without repeating is 'abc' with length 3.
hints: Use a sliding window with a HashSet or character frequency array.
Expand right pointer; when a repeat is found, shrink left pointer.
Use a HashMap to store the last seen index of each character for O(1) jumps.
title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Codes

constraints:
0 ≤ s.length ≤ 2000
0 ≤ p.length ≤ 2000
s and p contain only lowercase letters, '?', and '*'
description: ServiceNow's incident filter supports wildcard patterns where '?' matches any single character and '*' matches any sequence of characters (including empty). Given a pattern p and string s, determine if s matches p.
explanation: 'a*' matches 'aa': 'a' matches first 'a', '*' matches 'a'.
hints: Define dp[i][j] = true if s[0..i-1] matches p[0..j-1].
If p[j-1] == '*': dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (star matches one more char) OR dp[i][j-1] (star matches empty).
If p[j-1] is a letter: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] && s[i-1] == p[j-1].
Base: dp[0][0]=true; dp[0][j]=true only if all of p[0..j-1] are '*'.
title: Wildcard Matching for Incident Filters

description: Services in ServiceNow have dependencies forming a DAG. Each service has a resolution time. Find the longest path (maximum total time) from any source service to any sink service in the DAG.
hints: Compute topological order of the DAG.
Use DP in topological order: dp[v] = time[v] + max(dp[u]) for all predecessors u.
Initialize dp[v] = time[v] for source nodes.
Process nodes in topological order.
Answer is max(dp[v]) over all nodes.
title: Service Dependency Chain Longest Path

description: You are given an n×n matrix where each row and column is sorted in ascending order. Find the k-th smallest element in the matrix.
hints: Use a min-heap initialized with first element of each row (or first column).
Pop the minimum k times; each time push the next element from that element's row.
Alternatively binary search on the value range.
For binary search: count elements ≤ mid using a two-pointer scan (O(n) per count).
Find the smallest value such that exactly k elements ≤ value.
title: K-th Smallest Resolution Time

constraints:
1 ≤ articles ≤ 5000
1 ≤ keywords per article ≤ 20
1 ≤ keyword.length ≤ 30
1 ≤ prefix.length ≤ 30
description: Implement a multi-level keyword index for ServiceNow's knowledge base. Support: addArticle(List<String> keywords, int articleId) and searchByPrefix(String prefix) → return all article IDs whose keyword list contains at least one keyword with the given prefix, sorted by article ID.
explanation: Articles 1 and 3 have keywords starting with 'pass' (password).
hints: Build a Trie of keywords. Each leaf/end node stores a set of article IDs.
During insertion, at every Trie node along the keyword path, store the article ID.
For searchByPrefix, traverse to the prefix node, then DFS/BFS all subtree nodes.
Collect all article IDs found in the subtree and return them sorted.
Using sets at each node avoids duplicate article IDs.
title: Multi-Level Knowledge Base Search

constraints:
1 ≤ n ≤ 1000
edges.length == n
All edge values are in range [1,n]
description: Given a graph of n nodes labeled 1 to n, and a list of edges added one by one, find the last edge that creates a cycle (redundant connection). Return that edge. If multiple answers, return the one appearing last in the input.
explanation: Adding [2,3] creates a cycle 1-2-3-1.
hints: Use Union-Find. Process edges one by one.
For each edge [u,v], check if u and v are already in the same component.
If yes, this edge creates a cycle — return it.
If no, union the two components.
The first edge that connects two already-connected nodes is the answer.
title: Redundant Connection in Service Dependency Graph

description: Given an 8-bit unsigned integer representing part of a VM memory address, swap the lower 4 bits (nibble) with the upper 4 bits and return the result.
title: Swap Nibbles in VM Address

description: Given an array of free memory block sizes and a request size r, find the index of the smallest block that is at least r in size (best fit strategy). Return -1 if no block is large enough.
explanation: Blocks >= 3: [4,8,5] at indices [0,2,4]. Smallest is 4 at index 0.
title: VM Memory Allocation Best Fit

description: You are given an array height[] representing memory slot capacities. Find two slots i and j (i < j) that together form a container holding the maximum amount of virtual memory (min(height[i], height[j]) * (j - i)).
hints: Use two pointers starting at both ends.
Current capacity = min(height[left], height[right]) * (right - left).
Move the pointer pointing to the shorter height inward.
This greedy choice maximizes the potential for a larger container.
title: Container With Most Virtual Memory

description: A virtual network is represented as an undirected graph. Determine if the network is bipartite (can be partitioned into two groups such that every edge connects a node in one group to a node in the other). Return true if bipartite, false otherwise.
explanation: Color nodes alternately: 0→red, 1→blue, 2→red, 3→blue. No two adjacent nodes share a color.
hints: Use BFS/DFS with 2-coloring.
Assign color 0 to source, color 1 to neighbors, alternating.
If you find an edge where both endpoints have the same color, return false.
Handle disconnected components by starting BFS from every unvisited node.
title: Virtual Network Partition Detection

description: Given a VM configuration string, find the longest palindromic substring. A palindrome reads the same forward and backward.
explanation: "bab" is a palindrome of length 3. ("aba" is also valid.)
hints: Expand from center: for each character (and each pair of characters), expand outward while characters match.
Track the longest palindrome found.
Two center types: odd-length (single char center) and even-length (pair center).
DP approach: dp[i][j] = true if s[i..j] is palindrome.
title: Longest Palindromic Substring in VM Config

description: Data center resources are modeled as a flow network. VMs need to migrate from source datacenter to target datacenter. Each link has a migration bandwidth capacity. Find the maximum number of VMs (flow units) that can migrate simultaneously.
explanation: Maximum flow from 0 to 5 = 15 using multiple augmenting paths.
hints: Use Edmonds-Karp (BFS-based Ford-Fulkerson) for max flow.
Build a residual graph with backward edges initialized to 0.
Each BFS finds a shortest augmenting path.
Augment by the bottleneck (min capacity on path) and update residual graph.
Total flow = sum of all augmentations.
title: Network Flow for Virtual Machine Migration

constraints:
1 ≤ s.length ≤ 20
1 ≤ p.length ≤ 30
s contains only lowercase letters; p contains lowercase letters, '.', '*'
description: Implement regular expression matching with '.' (matches any single character) and '*' (matches zero or more of the preceding character). Given a string s and pattern p, return true if s matches the entire pattern p.
explanation: c* matches empty, a* matches 'aa', b matches 'b'. Full match.
hints: Define dp[i][j] = true if s[0..i-1] matches p[0..j-1].
If p[j-1] == '*': dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-2] (zero occurrences) OR (s[i-1] matches p[j-2] AND dp[i-1][j]).
If p[j-1] is a letter: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] && s[i-1] == p[j-1].
Initialize dp[0][0]=true and handle patterns like a* at dp[0][j].
title: Regular Expression Matching for VM Logs

description: You are given k sorted linked lists representing event logs from k virtual machines. Merge all lists into one sorted linked list. Return the merged list head.
explanation: Use a min-heap to always pick the smallest current head across all lists.
hints: Initialize min-heap with the first node of each non-empty list.
Pop the minimum node, add it to result, and push its next node (if any) to heap.
Store (val, listIndex, nodeRef) in heap to break ties and track position.
Repeat until heap is empty.
This runs in O(N log k) where N is total nodes.
title: Merge K Sorted VM Event Logs

constraints:
1 ≤ m, n ≤ 6
1 ≤ word.length ≤ 15
grid and word consist of uppercase and lowercase English letters
description: Given an m×n grid of characters and a target word, determine if the word exists in the grid. The word can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically), where the same cell may not be used more than once.
hints: Try starting DFS from every cell in the grid.
At each step, check if current cell matches current character in word.
Mark cell as visited (e.g., temporarily change character) to avoid reuse.
Explore all 4 directions recursively.
Restore cell character after DFS returns (backtracking).
title: Word Search in VMware Documentation Grid

description: You have n virtual machines to provision. Worker i can provision a VM in time[i] minutes. With k workers, find the minimum time T such that all n VMs can be provisioned within T minutes. Each worker can provision floor(T/time[i]) VMs in time T.
explanation: With 3 workers each provisioning floor(9/3)=3 VMs, total=9. T=9 suffices.
hints: Binary search on the answer T (time).
Lower bound: 1; upper bound: min(time) * n.
For a given T, total VMs provisionable = sum of floor(T/time[i]) for all workers.
If total >= n, T is feasible.
Find the minimum T where total >= n.
title: Minimum Time to Complete VM Provisioning

description: Given an array of integers, find the second largest distinct element. Return -1 if it does not exist.
explanation: Largest = 20. Second largest = 15.
title: Second Largest Element

description: Given a string representing an employee's full name, count the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u — case insensitive).
explanation: Vowels: a, e, u, a → 4. (Case insensitive)
title: Count Vowels in Employee Name

description: Given an integer array, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one number) with the largest sum and return its sum.
explanation: Subarray [4,-1,2,1] has the maximum sum = 6.
hints: Maintain currentMax and globalMax.
currentMax = max(nums[i], currentMax + nums[i]).
Update globalMax = max(globalMax, currentMax) at each step.
title: Kadane's Algorithm — Maximum Subarray

description: Given the head of a linked list, determine if it contains a cycle (a node's next pointer points back to an earlier node). Return true if a cycle exists, false otherwise.
explanation: The tail connects back to node at index 1, forming a cycle.
hints: Use Floyd's cycle detection (tortoise and hare).
Slow pointer moves 1 step, fast pointer moves 2 steps.
If they meet, a cycle exists.
title: Detect Cycle in Employee Reference List

description: Given a string containing just '(', ')', '{', '}', '[', ']', determine if the input string is valid. Valid means brackets are correctly opened and closed in the right order.
explanation: All brackets properly nested and closed.
hints: Use a stack.
Push opening brackets.
On closing bracket, pop and check if it matches.
title: Balanced Parentheses Validator

description: You have n projects each requiring some resource units and delivering a profit. Given a total resource budget W, select projects (each at most once) to maximize total profit without exceeding budget.
explanation: Select projects 2 and 3 (weights 20+30=50, values 100+120=220).
hints: Classic 0/1 Knapsack. Define dp[j] = max value with capacity j.
Iterate items outer, capacity inner (decreasing to prevent reuse).
dp[j] = max(dp[j], dp[j-w[i]] + v[i]) for j from W down to w[i].
Answer is dp[W].
Time: O(n*W), Space: O(W).
title: 0/1 Knapsack Resource Allocation

constraints:
2 ≤ n ≤ 15
graph[i] is a sorted list of nodes reachable from node i
description: Given a directed acyclic graph (DAG) with n nodes, find all possible paths from node 0 to node n-1. Return all paths in any order.
explanation: Paths from 0 to 3: 0→1→3 and 0→2→3.
hints: Use DFS with backtracking.
Maintain current path.
When node n-1 is reached, add current path to results.
Explore all neighbors recursively.
Backtrack by removing the last node after returning from recursion.
title: Find All Paths from Source to Target

description: Given a binary tree, find the maximum path sum. A path is a sequence of nodes where each pair of adjacent nodes has an edge. The path can start and end at any node. Path sum is the sum of node values along the path.
explanation: Path 15→20→7 has sum 42.
hints: Use DFS returning max gain from each subtree.
At each node, candidate max path = node.val + max(0, leftGain) + max(0, rightGain).
Update global max with this candidate.
Return node.val + max(0, leftGain, rightGain) to parent.
Negative gains should be excluded (treated as 0).
title: Maximum Path Sum in Binary Tree

description: Given an integer n, return an array ans of size n+1 where ans[i] is the number of 1-bits in the binary representation of i. Solve in O(n) time.
explanation: 0→0, 1→1, 2→1, 3→2, 4→1, 5→2 set bits respectively.
hints: Use DP: dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1).
This works because i >> 1 drops the last bit and i & 1 checks the last bit.
Or: dp[i] = dp[i & (i-1)] + 1 (i & (i-1) clears the lowest set bit).
Both approaches give O(n) time and O(n) space.
Initialize dp[0] = 0.
title: Number of 1 Bits in All Numbers from 1 to N

description: Given an array containing n distinct numbers taken from 0 to n, find the one number that is missing.
explanation: n=3, expected sum=0+1+2+3=6. Actual sum=4. Missing=6-4=2.
title: Missing Number in Sequence

description: Given a string s containing words separated by spaces, return the string with words reversed. Multiple spaces between words should be reduced to a single space; no leading or trailing spaces.
explanation: Split by spaces, filter empty strings, reverse list, join with single space.
title: Reverse Words in a Sentence

description: Given an array with values 0, 1, and 2, sort it in-place so all 0s come first, then 1s, then 2s. Use one pass with constant extra space.
explanation: Dutch National Flag: three pointers low, mid, high.
hints: Maintain three pointers: low=0, mid=0, high=n-1.
If nums[mid]==0: swap with nums[low], advance both low and mid.
If nums[mid]==1: advance mid.
If nums[mid]==2: swap with nums[high], decrement high (do not advance mid).
title: Sort Colors (Dutch National Flag)

description: Given the root of a binary tree, return its inorder traversal (left, root, right) using an iterative approach (no recursion).
explanation: Inorder: left→root→right. Use a stack to simulate recursion.
hints: Use a stack and a current pointer starting at root.
Push nodes to stack while going left.
Pop, visit, then move to right child.
Continue until stack and current are both empty/null.
title: Inorder Traversal Without Recursion

description: Given dimensions of a sequence of matrices, find the minimum number of scalar multiplications needed to compute their product. Matrix chain is given as array p where matrix i has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i].
hints: Use interval DP: dp[i][j] = min multiplications for chain from i to j.
dp[i][j] = min over k from i to j-1 of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j].
Base: dp[i][i] = 0 (single matrix, no multiplication).
Fill for increasing chain lengths.
Answer is dp[1][n-1] where n = len(p).
title: Matrix Chain Multiplication

description: Given an n×m binary grid where 0 is open and 1 is an obstacle, find the shortest path from top-left (0,0) to bottom-right (n-1,m-1). You may eliminate at most k obstacles. Return the length of the shortest path or -1 if none.
explanation: With k=1 elimination, shortest path has length 6.
hints: Use BFS where state = (row, col, k_remaining).
3D visited array: visited[r][c][k].
Start: (0,0,k). Target: (n-1,m-1,*).
When moving to a 1-cell, decrease k by 1 (only if k>0).
First time reaching target is the shortest path.
title: Shortest Path in a Grid with Obstacles

description: Given an n×n adjacency matrix isConnected[][] where isConnected[i][j]=1 means cities i and j are directly connected, find the total number of provinces (groups of directly/indirectly connected cities).
explanation: Cities 0 and 1 are connected, city 2 is isolated. 2 provinces.
hints: Use Union-Find or DFS/BFS.
For each pair (i,j) where isConnected[i][j]=1, union i and j.
Count distinct root representatives at the end.
Or DFS from each unvisited city and mark all reachable cities.
Number of DFS launches = number of provinces.
title: Number of Provinces

description: Given an array of integers, find the maximum product of any two distinct elements. Return (max1-1)*(max2-1) where max1 and max2 are the two largest values.
title: Maximum Product of Two Elements

description: Given a string, determine if it is a palindrome considering only alphanumeric characters and ignoring case.
explanation: After filtering non-alphanumeric and lowercasing: 'amanaplanacanalpanama'. It's a palindrome.
sampleInput: s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"

testCases.input: s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"
s="A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"

title: Check Palindrome String

description: Given a rotated sorted array (previously sorted, rotated at an unknown pivot) and a target value, find the target's index. Return -1 if not found. Solve in O(log n).
explanation: Binary search: determine which half is sorted, check if target in sorted half, else search other half.
hints: Standard binary search won't work directly on a rotated array.
At each step, determine which half is sorted: if nums[lo] ≤ nums[mid], left half is sorted.
Check if target is in the sorted half and narrow search accordingly.
title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array

constraints:
1 ≤ x ≤ 9
At most 100 calls to push, pop, top, empty
All pop/top calls made on non-empty stack
description: Implement a stack (LIFO) using only two queues. Implement push(x), pop(), top(), and empty() operations.
explanation: After push 1 and 2: top=2. Pop returns 2. Stack has [1], not empty.
hints: On push, enqueue to q2, then move all elements from q1 to q2, then swap q1 and q2.
This keeps q1 in LIFO order (latest element at front).
Pop and top simply operate on the front of q1.
title: Implement Stack Using Two Queues

description: Given an array where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position, determine if you can reach the last index starting from index 0.
explanation: Jump 1 step from 0 to 1, then 3 steps to end.
hints: Maintain the maximum reachable index.
For each index i, if i > maxReach, return false.
Update maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]).
If maxReach >= n-1, return true.
title: Jump Game — Can You Reach the End?

description: Given coin denominations and a target amount, find the minimum number of coins needed to make exactly that amount. Coins can be used unlimited times.
explanation: 11 = 5+5+1. Three coins.
hints: Unbounded knapsack DP. dp[i] = min coins for amount i.
dp[0] = 0; all others initialized to infinity.
dp[i] = min(dp[i-c]+1) for each coin c where c ≤ i.
Return dp[amount] if not infinity, else -1.
Iterate amount from 1 to target, inner loop over coins.
title: Coin Change — Minimum Coins

constraints:
1 ≤ n ≤ 100
graph[i] lists neighbors of node i
description: Given an undirected graph, check if it is bipartite. A graph is bipartite if nodes can be divided into two independent sets A and B such that every edge connects a node in A to a node in B.
explanation: 2-coloring: {0,2}=red, {1,3}=blue. Every edge goes between sets.
hints: BFS 2-coloring.
Assign color 0 to first node, alternate colors for neighbors.
If neighbor already has same color, not bipartite.
Handle disconnected components by starting from every uncolored node.
Odd-length cycles make a graph non-bipartite.
title: Bipartite Graph Check using BFS

constraints:
1 ≤ s.length ≤ 500
s consists of lowercase English letters
description: Given a string s, rearrange its characters so no two adjacent characters are the same. Return any valid rearrangement, or empty string if impossible.
explanation: Greedy: always pick the most frequent character that differs from last placed.
hints: Count frequency of each character.
Use a max-heap (priority queue) sorted by frequency.
At each step, pop the most frequent character.
If it equals the last placed character, pop the second most frequent, then push back.
If heap has only one element and it equals last placed, return empty string.
title: Reorganize String to Avoid Adjacent Repeats

constraints:
1 ≤ candidates.length ≤ 30
2 ≤ candidates[i] ≤ 40
All candidates are distinct
1 ≤ target ≤ 40
description: Given an array of distinct positive integers and a target, find all unique combinations that sum to target. Numbers may be used unlimited times.
explanation: 2+2+3=7 and 7=7 are the only combinations.
hints: Sort candidates to enable early termination.
Backtrack: at each step, include candidate[i] (may reuse) or skip to i+1.
Subtract chosen candidate from remaining target.
If remaining == 0, record combination.
Prune: if candidate[i] > remaining, stop (sorted array).
title: Combination Sum

description: Given an integer array where each element is between 1 and n (n = array length), find all elements that appear twice. Return them in any order. Solve without extra space.
explanation: Use index-marking: for each nums[i], negate nums[abs(nums[i])-1]. If already negative, it's a duplicate.
title: Find All Duplicates in Array

description: Given head of linked list L0→L1→...→Ln, reorder it to L0→Ln→L1→Ln-1→... in-place without modifying node values.
explanation: Find middle, reverse second half, merge two halves alternately.
hints: Find middle of list using slow/fast pointers.
Reverse the second half of the list.
Merge the two halves alternately.
title: Reorder Linked List

description: Given an array of daily temperatures, return an array answer where answer[i] is the number of days until a warmer temperature. If there is no future warmer day, answer[i] = 0.
explanation: Use a monotonic decreasing stack of indices.
hints: Maintain a stack of indices of temperatures waiting for a warmer day.
For each temperature, pop all stack indices with lower temperature.
answer[popped_index] = current_index - popped_index.
Push current index to stack.
title: Daily Temperatures — Next Warmer Day

description: Given the root of a BST and an integer k, return the kth smallest value (1-indexed) among all nodes.
explanation: Inorder traversal of BST gives sorted order: [1,2,3,4]. 1st smallest = 1.
hints: Inorder traversal (left, root, right) of BST gives elements in sorted order.
Count nodes visited; return when count equals k.
Can use iterative inorder with a stack.
title: Kth Smallest Element in BST

constraints:
1 ≤ beginWord.length ≤ 10
All words have the same length
1 ≤ wordList.length ≤ 5000
description: Given beginWord, endWord, and a wordList, find the minimum number of transformations to go from beginWord to endWord where each transformation changes exactly one letter and the intermediate word must be in wordList. Return 0 if no path.
explanation: hit→hot→dot→dog→cog = 5 words = 4 transformations + 1 = 5 (counting words).
hints: Use BFS where each word is a node.
Two words are connected if they differ by exactly one character.
Use a word set for O(1) lookups.
Generate all possible one-letter variations of current word and check if in set.
BFS level = number of transformations.
title: Word Ladder Minimum Transformations

description: Given a rod of length n and a price table price[i] (price of rod of length i), determine the maximum profit obtainable by cutting the rod into pieces and selling them.
explanation: Cut into two pieces of length 2: 5+5=10. Better than 9 (full rod) or 1+8=9 or 1+1+5=7.
hints: Unbounded knapsack variant.
dp[i] = max profit from rod of length i.
dp[i] = max over j from 1 to i of (price[j] + dp[i-j]).
Base case dp[0] = 0.
Answer is dp[n].
title: Maximum Profit from Rod Cutting

description: Given a string that may contain duplicate characters, return all distinct permutations.
explanation: 3 distinct permutations. Sort first and use a used[] array to skip duplicate branches.
hints: Sort the character array.
Use backtracking with a boolean used[] array.
Skip if nums[i]==nums[i-1] and !used[i-1] (avoid duplicate branches at same level).
Add to result when current permutation has length n.
This prunes duplicate permutations efficiently.
title: Permutations of String with Duplicates

constraints:
1 ≤ nums.length ≤ 10^5
k is in [1, distinct elements count]
description: Given an integer array and integer k, return the k most frequent elements. If two elements have the same frequency, return the one that appeared first in the array. You may return in any order.
explanation: 1 appears 3 times, 2 appears 2 times, 3 appears 1 time. Top 2: [1,2].
hints: Count frequency using HashMap.
Use a min-heap of size k: push (freq, num) pairs.
When heap size exceeds k, pop the minimum frequency element.
Remaining heap contains the k most frequent elements.
Alternatively use bucket sort: create buckets where index = frequency.
title: Find K Most Frequent Elements

description: Given an integer array and a target, find all unique quadruplets [a,b,c,d] such that a+b+c+d = target.
explanation: Sort array. Fix two outer indices, use two pointers for the remaining pair.
hints: Sort the array.
Use two nested loops for first two elements (i, j), then two pointers for remaining pair.
Skip duplicate values at each level to avoid duplicate quadruplets.
Inner two-pointer: move left right if sum too small, right left if too large.
Use long to avoid integer overflow when computing sums.
title: 4Sum — All Unique Quadruplets

description: Given a string s and a dictionary of words, return all possible ways to break s into a space-separated sequence of dictionary words.
explanation: Two valid segmentations of 'catsanddog'.
hints: Build a Trie from the dictionary for fast prefix lookup.
Use DFS/backtracking from each position in s.
At each position, traverse Trie to find all valid dictionary words starting here.
Recurse on the remaining suffix.
Memoize: cache results for each starting position to avoid recomputation.

testCases.output: ["pine apple pen apple","pine applepen apple","pineapple pen apple"]

title: Word Break II — All Segmentations

description: You are climbing a staircase with n steps. Each time you can climb 1, 2, or 3 steps. In how many distinct ways can you reach the top?
hints: This is Tribonacci: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] + dp[i-3].
Use bottom-up DP or memoization.
title: Climbing Stairs with Variable Jumps

description: Given a binary matrix where 1 = land and 0 = sea, count the number of land cells that cannot reach the boundary through adjacent (4-directional) land cells.
explanation: Land cells at (1,2),(2,1),(2,2) are enclosed. (1,0) is boundary-connected.
hints: Run DFS/BFS from all boundary land cells, marking them.
Count remaining unmarked land cells.
These are the enclaves.
title: Number of Enclaves

description: Find the contiguous subarray of length k with the maximum average value. Return the maximum average.
explanation: Subarray [12,-5,-6,50] has sum=51, average=51/4=12.75.
hints: Compute initial window sum for first k elements.
Slide window: add new element, subtract leftmost.
Track maximum sum seen, return maxSum/k.
title: Maximum Average Subarray

description: A robot is on an m×n grid. It can only move right or down. Some cells contain obstacles (1=obstacle, 0=empty). Count the number of unique paths from top-left to bottom-right.
explanation: Two paths: right-right-down-down and down-down-right-right (obstacle blocks center).
hints: dp[i][j] = number of ways to reach (i,j).
If obstacle, dp[i][j]=0.
Else dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1].
Base: dp[0][0]=1 if no obstacle, dp[0][j]=dp[0][j-1] if no obstacle.
Handle obstacles in first row/column.
title: Unique Paths in a Grid with Obstacles

description: Given a dictionary of root words and a sentence, replace each word in the sentence with its shortest root from the dictionary. If no root exists, keep the word as is.
explanation: cattle→cat (root), rattled→rat, battery→bat.
hints: Build a Trie from the dictionary roots.
For each word in the sentence, traverse the Trie.
Return the root when an end-of-word marker is found.
If no root found, keep original word.
This is more efficient than checking each word against all roots.
sampleInput: dictionary = ["cat","bat","rat"], sentence = "the cattle was rattled by the battery"
sampleOutput: "the cat was rat by the bat"

testCases.input: dictionary = ["cat","bat","rat"], sentence = "the cattle was rattled by the battery"
testCases.output: "the cat was rat by the bat"

testCases.input: dictionary=["cat","bat","rat"], sentence="the cattle was rattled by the battery"
testCases.output: "the cat was rat by the bat"

title: Replace Words with Root in Dictionary

description: Given n points on a 2D plane, connect all points with minimum total Manhattan distance. Find the minimum cost (sum of edge weights in MST using Manhattan distance).
explanation: MST using Manhattan distances between all pairs gives total cost 20.
hints: Prim's algorithm is efficient here: start from any node.
Use a min-heap or maintain minDist[] array.
At each step, pick the point with minimum Manhattan distance to current MST.
Update minDist for all unvisited points based on the newly added point.
Total cost = sum of all chosen edge weights.
title: Minimum Cost to Connect All Points

description: Same as the classic Task Scheduler problem. Find minimum time to run n tasks with at most k same-task cooldown. Return the minimum number of intervals.
explanation: A→B→C→A→B→idle→A = 7 intervals.
hints: Count frequency of each task type.
Use a max-heap to always run the most frequent available task.
Use a cooldown queue to hold tasks that are in cooldown.
At each time unit: pop from heap (run task), add to cooldown queue.
Release tasks from cooldown queue when cooldown expires, push back to heap.
title: Rearrange Tasks at Minimum Intervals

description: For a given array, the XOR sum of a subset is the XOR of all its elements. Find the sum of XOR sums of all possible subsets.
hints: Each bit position is independent.
For bit b: it contributes to XOR sum of a subset only if XOR of subset has bit b set.
Key insight: if any number has bit b set, then exactly 2^(n-1) subsets have XOR sum with bit b set.
Total sum = OR of all elements * 2^(n-1).
This O(n) formula works because each set bit in OR(all nums) contributes 2^(n-1) to the answer.
title: Subset XOR Sum of All Subsets

description: Given an integer array, move all zeros to the end while maintaining the relative order of non-zero elements. Do this in-place.
explanation: Two pointer: keep a slow pointer for next non-zero position. Sweep with fast pointer.
title: Move Zeros to End

constraints:
1 ≤ nums.length ≤ 3×10^4
-10^4 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^4
Array is sorted non-decreasingly
description: Given a sorted array, remove duplicates in-place such that each unique element appears at most twice. Return the new length k. The first k elements of the modified array should contain the result.
explanation: Modified array: [1,1,2,2,3,...]. Length 5.
hints: Use a slow pointer k (write index).
For each element, allow it if k < 2 or nums[i] > nums[k-2].
Copy eligible elements to nums[k++].
title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II

description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the values of nodes visible from the right side (rightmost node at each level).
explanation: Level 0: [1], Level 1: [2,3], Level 2: [5,4]. Rightmost at each level: 1,3,4.
hints: BFS level-order traversal.
At each level, the last node processed is the rightmost visible node.
Record the value of the last node in each BFS level.
title: Right Side View of Binary Tree

description: Given an integer array and integer k, return the number of contiguous subarrays whose sum is divisible by k.
explanation: Use prefix sum mod k. If two prefixes have same mod, subarray between them is divisible by k.
hints: Compute prefix sums modulo k.
If prefix[i] mod k == prefix[j] mod k, then sum(i+1..j) is divisible by k.
Use HashMap to count prefix mod frequencies.
Initialize map with {0:1} (empty prefix).
Handle negative remainders: ((prefix % k) + k) % k.
title: Subarray Sum Divisible by K

description: Given a nested list of integers, implement an iterator to flatten it. Each element is either an integer or a list whose elements may also be integers or lists. Implement hasNext() and next().
explanation: Flatten recursively: [1,1,2,1,1].
hints: Use a stack to simulate DFS iteratively.
Push all elements in reverse order initially.
hasNext: while top is a list, pop it and push its elements in reverse.
next: call hasNext first, then pop and return the top integer.
Alternatively, fully flatten in constructor using DFS.
title: Flatten Nested List Iterator

description: Given a string s and a list of pairs [i,j] meaning you can swap s[i] and s[j] any number of times, return the lexicographically smallest string possible after any number of swaps.
explanation: Swap (0,3): 'bcad'. Swap (1,2): 'bacd'. Or swap (1,2) first: 'dcba', then (0,3): 'abcd'... Actually 'abcd' is smaller. Union components: {0,3},{1,2}. Sort within each component's positions: {0,3}→'a','d' positions, {1,2}→'b','c' positions sorted lexicographically.
hints: Use Union-Find to group all connected positions.
Positions in the same component can be rearranged in any order.
For each component, collect the characters at those positions.
Sort the characters and assign them back to sorted positions.
Construct result string from these assignments.
title: Smallest String with Swaps

constraints:
1 ≤ s.length ≤ 10^5
s consists of lowercase English letters
description: Given a string, find the first character that does not repeat. Return its index, or -1 if no such character exists.
explanation: 'l' at index 0 is the first non-repeating character.
title: First Non-Repeating Character

description: Each child has a greed factor g[i] (minimum cookie size needed). Each cookie has size s[j]. Assign at most one cookie per child to maximize number of satisfied children. A child is satisfied if cookie size >= greed factor.
explanation: Only one cookie of size 1 can satisfy child with greed=1.
hints: Sort both g and s.
Use two pointers: try to match smallest sufficient cookie to least greedy unsatisfied child.
If s[j] >= g[i], satisfy child i and advance both pointers.
title: Assign Cookies to Children

description: Given a rotated sorted array that may contain duplicates, find the minimum element. This is harder than the no-duplicates version.
explanation: When nums[mid] == nums[hi], we can't determine which half is sorted. Simply decrement hi.
hints: Standard approach: compare nums[mid] with nums[hi].
If nums[mid] < nums[hi]: min is in left half (hi=mid).
If nums[mid] > nums[hi]: min is in right half (lo=mid+1).
If nums[mid] == nums[hi]: can't tell, just hi-- (worst case O(n)).
title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II

description: Given a 2D binary matrix of '0's and '1's, find the largest square containing only '1's and return its area.
explanation: Largest square of 1s has side 2, area=4.
hints: dp[i][j] = side length of largest square with bottom-right corner at (i,j).
If matrix[i][j]=='1': dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1.
If matrix[i][j]=='0': dp[i][j] = 0.
Track maximum dp[i][j]; answer = max^2.
Space can be optimized to O(cols) using previous row.
title: Maximal Square

description: Given an array of bus routes where routes[i] contains stops served by bus i, find the minimum number of buses to travel from source stop S to target stop T. Return -1 if impossible.
explanation: Take bus 0 (stops 1,2,7), then take bus 1 (stops 3,6,7). 2 buses.
hints: BFS where nodes are bus routes (not stops).
Map each stop to all bus routes serving it.
Start: enqueue all routes containing stop S.
BFS: for each route, visit all stops; for each stop, enqueue all new routes.
When target stop T is found, return current BFS level.
title: Bus Routes — Minimum Number of Buses

description: Given an integer array and k, return the number of contiguous subarrays with exactly k distinct integers. Solve in O(n).
hints: Exact k = atMost(k) - atMost(k-1).
AtMost(k) counts subarrays with at most k distinct integers.
Use sliding window for atMost(k): shrink left when distinct count exceeds k.
Count += right - left + 1 at each step.
Subtract atMost(k-1) from atMost(k) for exact count.
title: Subarrays with K Different Integers

description: Given a binary string, split it into two non-empty substrings. The score = number of '0's in left + number of '1's in right. Return the maximum score over all possible splits.
explanation: Split at index 3: left='011'(one 0), right='101'(two 1s). Score=1+2=3. Better split: index 1: left='0'(one 0), right='11101'(four 1s). Score=1+4=5.
hints: Precompute prefix sum of '0's from left and suffix sum of '1's from right.
For each split point i from 1 to n-1, score = zeros_left[i] + ones_right[i].
Track the maximum score.
Alternatively compute on-the-fly with O(1) space: track zeros and ones counts as you scan.
Total_ones - ones_seen_so_far + zeros_seen = formula for each split.
title: Maximum Score After Splitting String

description: Given an integer array, find the total Hamming distance between all pairs of integers. Hamming distance between two integers is the number of positions where corresponding bits differ.
hints: For each bit position (0-31), count ones (c) and zeros (n-c).
Contribution of this bit to total Hamming distance = c*(n-c).
Sum over all 32 bit positions.
This avoids O(n^2) pairwise comparison, giving O(32n) = O(n).
Total = sum over all bits of (count_ones * count_zeros).
title: Total Hamming Distance

description: Two nodes of a BST were swapped by mistake. Recover the BST without changing its structure. Solve in O(1) space (Morris traversal).
explanation: Nodes 2 and 3 were swapped. Inorder traversal reveals the two nodes out of order.
hints: Do inorder traversal; two nodes will be out of order.
First violation: prev > curr; first = prev, second = curr.
Second violation (if any): first stays; second = curr.
After traversal, swap values of first and second nodes.
For O(1) space, use Morris traversal (no stack/recursion).
title: Recover Binary Search Tree

description: You are given an empty m×n grid of water. Process a list of operations where each operation adds a land cell at position (r,c). After each operation, return the number of islands.
explanation: After each addition, islands form/merge based on adjacency.
hints: Use Union-Find. Each new land cell has up to 4 neighbors.
When merging with an existing island, decrement island count.
Track which cells are already land to avoid duplicate additions.
Union with each adjacent land cell and decrement count for each successful union.
Initial count for each new cell is 1; subtract for each merge.
title: Number of Islands II (Dynamic Island Addition)

description: Given a circular integer array, find the maximum possible sum of a non-empty subarray in O(n). A circular subarray can wrap around the ends.
explanation: Normal subarray [3] gives 3. Circular max = max(Kadane result, total_sum - min_subarray_sum).
hints: Case 1: Max subarray is non-circular → Kadane's algorithm.
Case 2: Max subarray wraps around → total_sum - minimum_subarray_sum.
Minimum subarray sum can also be found with Kadane's (negate array or track min).
Answer = max(case1, case2) unless all elements are negative.
Edge case: if all elements are negative, answer is max element (no wrapping).
title: Maximum Sum Circular Subarray

description: Given widths and heights of n bars, find the area of the largest rectangle that fits within any contiguous set of bars (standard histogram rectangle problem).
explanation: The rectangle spanning all 7 bars at height 2 = 14, but bars 2-6 (height 3) = 15, bars 3-5 (height 4) = 12. Bar 4 (height 5) = 5. Best = 3*5=15... recount: bars 1-5 (5 bars) at height 3 = 15. Bars 2-4 (3 bars) at height 4=12. Actually all 7 bars at height 2=14. Best is 15 or spans 2-4 h=4 giving 12. Max=15.
hints: Use a monotonic increasing stack of indices.
When a shorter bar is encountered, pop taller bars and compute area.
Width = current_index - stack_top_index - 1 (or current_index if stack empty).
Process each bar once, total O(n).
Add a sentinel 0 at the end to flush all remaining bars from stack.
title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram (TI Variant)

description: Given four integer arrays A,B,C,D each of length n, count how many tuples (i,j,k,l) exist such that A[i]+B[j]+C[k]+D[l] == 0.
explanation: Pairs: A[0]+B[0]+C[0]+D[1]=-2+1+2+(-1)=0 wait... use HashMappairing: precompute all A[i]+B[j], store in map, then check -(C[k]+D[l]).
hints: Divide into two groups: (A,B) and (C,D).
Store all A[i]+B[j] sums in a HashMap with their frequencies.
For each C[k]+D[l], look up -(C[k]+D[l]) in the map.
Add map count to result.
O(n^2) time and space.
title: 4Sum II — Count Tuples

description: A fence has n posts. You have k colors. Paint all posts such that no more than 2 adjacent posts have the same color. Count the number of ways.
explanation: same[i] = posts i and i-1 have same color; diff[i] = different. same[i]=(k-1)*diff[i-1], diff[i]=(k-1)*(same[i-1]+diff[i-1]). Total[3]=6.
hints: Track same[i] = ways where post i has same color as i-1.
diff[i] = ways where post i has different color from i-1.
same[i] = diff[i-1] * 1 (extend same pair, but same color as previous).
diff[i] = (same[i-1] + diff[i-1]) * (k-1).
Answer = same[n] + diff[n].
title: Paint Fence with K Colors

description: Given an integer array and integers low and high, count the number of pairs (i,j) where i<j and low ≤ nums[i] XOR nums[j] ≤ high.
hints: Use a Binary Trie.
countPairs(val) counts pairs with XOR < val using Trie.
Answer = countPairs(high+1) - countPairs(low).
Build Trie incrementally and count for each new number.
At each bit, check opposite bit path for XOR contribution.
title: Count Pairs with XOR in Range

description: Given a m×n grid filled with non-negative integers, find a path from top-left to bottom-right (only right or down moves) that minimizes the sum of numbers along the path.
hints: dp[i][j] = min path sum to reach (i,j).
dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
Handle first row (only right moves) and first column (only down moves) as base cases.
Fill table row by row.
dp[m-1][n-1] is the answer.
title: Minimum Path Sum in Grid

description: Given a positive integer target and array of positive integers, find the minimal length subarray whose sum is >= target. Return 0 if no such subarray.
explanation: Subarray [4,3] has sum 7 and length 2.
hints: Use sliding window with two pointers.
Expand right pointer; when sum >= target, try shrinking left.
Update minimum length whenever sum >= target.
Shrink left while sum >= target to minimize window.
O(n) time.
title: Minimum Size Subarray Sum

description: Given an integer array and integer k, return the number of contiguous subarrays with exactly k odd numbers.
explanation: Subarrays [1,1,2,1] and [1,2,1,1] each have 3 odd numbers.
hints: Transform: odd=1, even=0. Now find subarrays with sum==k.
Use prefix sum + HashMap.
Count prefix sums. For each index i, look for prefix[i]-k in map.
Initialize map with {0:1}.
This is identical to subarray sum equals k problem.
title: Count Number of Nice Subarrays

description: You are playing snakes and ladders on an n×n board (numbered 1 to n^2 in boustrophedon order). Given the board where -1 means no snake/ladder, find the minimum dice rolls to reach square n^2. Return -1 if impossible.
explanation: From square 1, roll 1 to reach square 2 which has ladder to square 3=n^2.
hints: BFS where each node is a board square.
From square s, you can roll 1-6 to reach s+1 to s+6.
If destination has snake/ladder (board != -1), jump to that destination.
Convert square number to (row,col) carefully using boustrophedon order.
Track visited squares; BFS level = minimum rolls.
title: Snakes and Ladders Shortest Path

description: A thief wants to rob houses arranged in a binary tree. Adjacent nodes (parent-child) cannot both be robbed. Maximize the total amount robbed.
explanation: Rob nodes 3,3,1=7. Can't rob parent and child together.
hints: For each node, return pair (rob_this, skip_this).
rob_this = node.val + skip_left + skip_right.
skip_this = max(rob_left,skip_left) + max(rob_right,skip_right).
Process bottom-up (post-order DFS).
Root answer = max(rob_root, skip_root).
title: House Robber III — Binary Tree

description: Given an integer array, you can jump from index i to i-1, i+1, or any index j where arr[i]==arr[j]. Find the minimum number of jumps to reach the last index from index 0.
explanation: 0→4 (same value 100)→3→9 (same value 404). 3 jumps.
hints: BFS where each index is a node.
Edges: i±1 and all indices with same value.
Use a HashMap mapping value to list of indices.
After visiting indices for a value, clear them from map to avoid revisiting.
BFS level = minimum jumps.
title: Jump Game IV — Minimum Jumps

description: Packages must be shipped in order within D days. Find the minimum ship capacity such that all packages can be shipped within D days.
explanation: Binary search on capacity. Min=max(weights), Max=sum(weights). For capacity 15, days needed=5.
hints: Binary search on capacity between max(weights) and sum(weights).
Check function: greedily assign consecutive packages to days, count days needed.
If days needed ≤ D, capacity is feasible.
Find minimum feasible capacity.
title: Capacity to Ship Packages

constraints:
2 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 10^5
-10^9 ≤ Node.val ≤ 10^9
p ≠ q, both p and q exist in BST
description: Given a BST and two nodes p and q, find their lowest common ancestor (LCA). The LCA is the deepest node that has both p and q as descendants.
explanation: 6 is the root; both 2 (left) and 8 (right) are its descendants.
hints: Use BST property: if both p and q are less than root, LCA is in left subtree.
If both are greater, LCA is in right subtree.
Otherwise root is the LCA.
title: Lowest Common Ancestor in BST

description: You have n product versions 1 to n. There is a function isBadVersion(k) that returns whether version k is bad. Find the first bad version with minimum API calls.
explanation: Binary search: call isBadVersion(mid). If true, search left; if false, search right.
hints: Binary search between 1 and n.
If isBadVersion(mid) is true, the first bad is at mid or earlier: hi=mid.
If false, first bad is after mid: lo=mid+1.
When lo==hi, that is the first bad version.
title: First Bad Version

description: Given a binary grid (1=land, 0=water), find the maximum area of any island. An island is formed by connecting adjacent 1-cells (4-directionally).
explanation: Island of size 3 (three connected 1s in top-right region).
hints: DFS from each unvisited 1-cell.
Count cells visited in each DFS call.
Mark cells visited to avoid revisiting.
Track maximum count across all DFS runs.
title: Max Area of Island

description: Given an integer x, return true if x is a palindrome, and false otherwise. An integer is a palindrome when it reads the same forward and backward.
explanation: 121 reads as 121 from left to right and from right to left.
hints: Negative numbers are never palindromes.
Consider edge cases and validate your approach.
Consider edge cases and validate your approach.
title: Palindrome Number

constraints:
2 ≤ numbers.length ≤ 3 * 10^4
-1000 ≤ numbers[i] ≤ 1000
-1000 ≤ target ≤ 1000
Exactly one solution exists.
description: Given a 1-indexed array of integers numbers that is already sorted in non-decreasing order, find two numbers such that they add up to a specific target number. Return the indices of the two numbers (1-indexed).
explanation: numbers[1] + numbers[2] = 2 + 7 = 9. Return [1, 2].
hints: Use two pointers from both ends.
Consider edge cases and validate your approach.
Consider edge cases and validate your approach.
title: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted

description: Given an integer n, return the number of prime numbers that are strictly less than n.
explanation: There are 4 prime numbers less than 10: 2, 3, 5, 7.
hints: Use the Sieve of Eratosthenes.
Consider edge cases and validate your approach.
Consider edge cases and validate your approach.
title: Count Primes

description: Given an integer array nums, find three numbers whose product is maximum and return the maximum product.
explanation: The maximum product is 2 * 3 * 4 = 24
hints: Consider both positive and negative numbers
The maximum product might involve the two smallest numbers if they're negative
title: Maximum Product of Three Numbers

0 <= k <= 50
s consists of English letters
description: Given a string s and an integer k, return the length of the longest substring of s that contains at most k distinct characters.
explanation: The substring is "ece" with length 3 and 2 distinct characters
hints: Use sliding window technique
Track character frequencies in current window
title: Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters

description: Given an array of positive integers nums and a positive integer target, return the minimal length of a contiguous subarray whose product is greater than or equal to target. If there is no such subarray, return 0.
explanation: The subarray [3,4] has the minimal length where the product is 12 >= 7
hints: Use two pointers approach
Shrink window when product exceeds target
title: Minimum Size Subarray Product

constraints:
1 <= n <= 2000
0 <= edges.length <= 5000
edges[i].length == 2
0 <= ai, bi < n
description: Given n nodes labeled from 0 to n-1 and a list of directed edges, find the number of strongly connected components in the graph.
explanation: Components are: {0,1,2}, {3}, {4}
hints: Use Kosaraju's or Tarjan's algorithm
First DFS to get finish times, then transpose graph
title: Count Connected Components in Directed Graph

The number of nodes in the tree is in range [1, 3000]
-100 <= Node.val <= 100
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the maximum width of the given tree. The width of a level is defined as the length between the leftmost and rightmost non-null nodes.
explanation: The maximum width is at level 3 with length 4 (5,3,null,9)
hints: Use BFS and track positions
Position of left child = 2*pos, right child = 2*pos+1

description: There are n houses and k different colors. The cost of painting each house with a certain color is represented by an n x k cost matrix. No two adjacent houses can have the same color. Find the minimum cost to paint all houses.
explanation: Paint house 0 with color 0 and house 1 with color 2. Total cost: 1 + 4 = 5
hints: Use dynamic programming
For each house, try all colors except the one used for previous house
title: Painting House with K Colors

description: Given arrival and departure times of meetings, find the maximum number of meetings that can be accommodated in a single room.
hints: Sort by end times
Select meeting with earliest end time that doesn't conflict
title: Maximum Meetings in One Room

1 <= s.length <= 20
s consists of digits only
description: Given a string containing only digits, return all possible valid IP addresses that can be obtained from the string.
explanation: All valid IP addresses formed from the string
hints: Use backtracking with 4 segments
Each segment must be 0-255 and no leading zeros except '0'
title: Generate All Valid IP Addresses

description: Given an integer array nums, return the maximum result of nums[i] XOR nums[j], where 0 <= i <= j < n.
explanation: The maximum XOR is 5 XOR 25 = 28
hints: Build a binary trie with bit representation
For each number, find the path that maximizes XOR
title: Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in Array

-10^4 <= nums[i] <= 10^4

explanation: After each add operation, return the kth largest element
hints: Use a min heap of size k
Maintain only k largest elements
title: Kth Largest Element in Stream

num consists of only digits
num does not have leading zeros except for zero itself
description: Given string num representing a non-negative integer and an integer k, remove k digits from the number so that the new number is the smallest possible.

hints: Use monotonic stack
Remove larger digits from left to keep smaller ones

All calls are in chronological order
At most 300 calls to hit and getHits

explanation: getHits returns count of hits in past 300 seconds

Remove timestamps older than 300 seconds
sampleInput: hit(1), hit(2), hit(3), getHits(4), hit(300), getHits(300), getHits(301)
title: Design Hit Counter

nums is rotated

explanation: The minimum element is 0
hints: Handle duplicates by reducing search space linearly when needed
Compare with right boundary
title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array with Duplicates

description: Given a positive integer n, count the total number of set bits in binary representation of all numbers from 1 to n.
hints: Find pattern in set bits contribution at each bit position
Use mathematical formula
title: Count Total Set Bits from 1 to N

n == matrix[i].length
1 <= m, n <= 200
-10^5 <= matrix[i][j] <= 10^5
0 <= row < m

description: Given a 2D matrix, handle multiple queries: update a cell value and calculate sum of elements inside a rectangle.
explanation: After update, sum of region is 5+0+1+6+6+3 = 20
hints: Use 2D Fenwick tree or segment tree
For simpler solution, recalculate on each query
title: Range Sum Query 2D - Mutable

description: Initially there are n provinces. Given operations to merge provinces, return the number of provinces after each operation.
explanation: After each merge, count of provinces reduces
hints: Use Union-Find with path compression
Track component count

constraints:
